data.title: Intel: Here Are 2 Reasons To Buy Ahead Of Next Earnings Call
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It's been a while since I last covered Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) here. If you read my last analysis, you’d know I'm bullish on Intel for one main reason: Lip-Bu Tan. This man is shredding all the excess fat of the company. He was cutting staff left and right while freeing up cash via a majority stake sale in Altera to Silver Lake. Yesterday, Tuesday, the stock jumped quite a bit, and of course, we have Tan to blame. Lip-Bu Tan has come out and laid the map down on the table, a map that is tipped to lead Intel back to chip dominance. The market has already reacted favourably to his new roadmap, and the stock shot up almost 9% amid news that curbs on exports to China should be somewhat reduced. After following their product lineup for a while now, I'm even more bullish on Intel, especially since the outlook was lowered during the last earnings. Intel is all set with a couple of shiny new GPUs, which should compete closely with Nvidia (NVDA), as I will explain. While on the CPU front, the Core 9 270H is hitting all the right checkpoints. This revamped lineup and more streamlined direction can only mean good news for an already undervalued stock. Because of this, I expect good news on the revenue front during the next earnings. Investors have between now and then to find their entry point into the stock.

A 9% Surge Tuesday

Intel investors had to be happy yesterday after seeing a powerful 9% rally. The stock opened up around $20.50 and got bumped up to $22.44. Tan has already initiated a handful of changes at Intel that he believes will put them on a more streamlined path to success. One goal is to reduce bureaucracy and cut out all the noise by trimming the workforce at Intel. He also plans to bring focus back on chip production, falling back on core engineering principles when it comes to growth. Despite this new enthusiasm, Intel’s 1Q25 earnings were not nearly impressive enough to give investors new hope. Revenue growth year over year remained flat as the company brought in $12.7 billion, and while they beat on EPS, guidance for 2Q25 was rather disappointing at $11.2–$12.4 billion. The stock fell roughly 5% after the call. Looking back at it now, that pullback on the 2Q outlook is the best thing Intel could have done; the stock has disappointed a lot, so now any small positivity around the chipmaker will be taken with open arms. And that's exactly what we started to see yesterday: a new optimism around the company's direction, as Tan brings focus back on a strong foundry business. Intel is doing everything right after what has been a tough couple of years; they now just need one big boost to bring them back, and it's coming in the next earnings.

Catching Nvidia on the AI front

If Intel wants to stay around in the future, they have to stay ahead of a rapidly changing AI industry. AI is here to stay and will no longer come in big booms. Rather, chipmakers must put in the effort to keep up with a changing future as new AI tech gets rolled out. Intel knows this; they've overhauled the AI leadership, promoting Sachin Katti to the newly created role of Chief Technology and AI Officer. The way I see it, this gives them the opportunity to split their data centre efforts from their AI group. Katti can now head a team specifically for keeping up with AI accelerators such as the Arc B60 and B50 GPUs. These are specifically made for AI work tasks such as chatbots, simulations, and big data, but also serve well for pro-visualization and inference-heavy applications. Both bring something different to the table; the B60 is a little more heavy-duty and runs 24 GB of memory, not bad at all for running big models. While the B50 runs on 16 GB of memory, it uses far less electricity and, being smaller, of course, can be shoved into tighter spaces. The B60 is also tipped to offer a fully loaded setup dubbed “Project Battlematrix”, a system with multiple B60s that can run massive AI models in-house instead of relying on cloud services like AWS. In designing these two graphic cards, Intel really wants to catch up with giants Nvidia, and the best way to compete on that front is the price. Both GPUs are part of a push to offer affordable, AI-ready alternatives to NVIDIA’s more expensive cards. You can see in the table I made below that Intel’s new cards should compete with mid-tier Nvidia AI cards like the RTX A6000 on performance, and at the same time, offer more VRAM per dollar. For me, the smaller B50 is a great match for Nvidia’s RTX 4000, both are 70W low-power cards, but Intel offers a better price for comparable AI inference power. The biggest takeaway from this table should be the rough price guide I give; Intel beat on both chips in terms of monetary value. The price estimates on the new Intel GPUs were sourced from Tom's Hardware.

Making the Weigh-in on the Mobile CPU Front

Intel’s new Core 9 270H, which was launched quite some time ago back at the end of 2024, has just now hit above its weight in terms of speed. Benchmark tests are out, and it’s tipped to be almost 14% faster than the Core i5; that's a big deal, 14% in terms of CPU speed is a hefty jump. I run an i5 in my gaming setup, and it's plenty fast enough for modern gaming standards, so the new Core 9 is really staying ahead of the game in terms of speed. Back in the day, if you wanted to run any heavy game, you'd need a well-built PC, but now that's not the case; more and more people are switching to laptops for powerful tasks. The 270H in a laptop will be a strong contender now when it comes to choice. The CPU is effectively a refreshed version of the Core i9-13900H, but now with much higher turbo speeds and better RAM support. If you’re familiar with their product range, you'd know that the 270H is the fastest mobile CPU in their Raptor Lake line.

One Big Push

After reading all about that fancy new tech I just explained, you’d think Intel tech is booming with no drawbacks. Well, while their new tech rollout is very impressive, we have to put both the tech and the stock price in context. The market continues to be skeptical about Intel’s future, with the stock trading sideways for the most part since last August. What I'm saying here is that this stock keeps hitting a ceiling; it's been stuck below the 50-week moving average. There have been a number of failed breakout attempts this year, and after each one, the ceiling is getting lower. Despite showing us all the signs of a company with strong fundamentals, what Intel needs is one piece of really good news. I think it's going to come next earnings.

1Q25 earnings were disappointing, but that's because they just came too soon for Tan’s new Intel to make an imprint on revenue. Remember, Tan has a long road to climb and is fighting a rundown and sloppy Intel on multiple fronts. He has to trim the fat by cutting jobs across the board, deal with an ever-changing AI scene, and ramp up the foundry business, all while dealing with tariffs in between. We, as investors, need to give Intel the time to sort through all this, and that time will be between now and the next earnings. The lower revenue guidance for 2Q sets them up perfectly to impress us. We’ve all been saying for some time now that the end of Windows support is approaching. I believe that just now we are entering into the territory in which PC sales will show up on revenue; last quarter was far too soon.

Valuation

Whichever way you look at it, Intel is cheap, even after yesterday's surge. Intel stock is still on a slow and long recovery back up to the numbers we saw a few years ago. Seeking Alpha shows a forward price-to-sales ratio of 1.91 against a sector median of 2.98. The forward EV to sales ratio sits at 2.61 against a median of 2.95. The market cap is around $96 billion. RSI is currently around 45, still an attractive number for investors.

What's Next?

Expect more of the same from Intel stock between now and next earnings. Shares could fall below $20 and may stay above $22, but I don’t see us crossing past $30 before the next print; instead, I think that’s what we may see because of the next print. You shouldn’t feel you’ve missed out on this one after the 9% rally yesterday; there will be other buying opportunities. As stated, I expect Intel to beat the next earnings. Tan is moving in the right direction. The fruits of his labour are yet to show up, but I remain bullish on Intel.

data.title: Repsol: The Outlook Remains Positive, Despite Low Oil Prices
data.description:
About six months ago, I recommended buying Repsol (OTCQX:REPYF) for its solid business model, its exceptionally cheap valuation and its attractive 8.6% dividend, which had a wide margin of safety. Since that article, the stock has outperformed the broad market and its sector by an impressive margin. To be sure, the stock has offered a total return of 24% in just six months, whereas the S&P 500 and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) have declined 1% and 6%, respectively. The key factors behind the vast outperformance of Repsol are the cheap initial valuation of the stock and a positive outlook of the natural gas market.

After such striking outperformance, it is only natural for investors to wonder whether they should take their profits and look elsewhere for attractive returns. This is especially true now that OPEC has begun to unwind its production cuts, thus driving the price of oil near a 4-year low. However, Repsol produces much more natural gas than oil and hence it is more resilient to the decline of the oil price than the well-known oil majors, such as Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). Given also the positive outlook of the natural gas market (more on this below), the stock remains attractive. Due to its rally, its yield has decreased from 8.6% to 7.6%, but it remains attractive for income-oriented investors.

Business Overview

Repsol is headquartered in Spain, and thus it passes under the radar of most U.S. investors. Most oil producers have been hurt by the sharp decline of the price of oil this year. This decline has resulted from the global economic turmoil caused by the announcement of tariffs by the new U.S. government but mostly from a major shift in the policy of OPEC.

The cartel provided strong support to the price of oil by maintaining deep production cuts in recent years, but this policy reached its limits, as it caused frustration to most OPEC members. Revenues from oil constitute a major component of the fiscal budget of the members of the cartel, and thus the low production quotas caused trouble for these countries. Even worse, OPEC members saw their sacrifices being wasted, at least in part, as the U.S., Canada and Brazil, which do not belong to the cartel, boosted their output and partly offset the effect of the production cuts of OPEC on the price of oil. This tough situation led OPEC to begin to unwind its production cuts in April, with a goal to boost its output by approximately 2.2 million barrels per day until the end of next year. As the price of oil is highly sensitive to the balance between global supply and demand, it is only natural that the price of oil has declined near a 4-year low level lately.

Repsol is inevitably hurt by low oil prices, but it is much more resilient than most of its peers. Its key difference from the well-known oil majors is its primary focus on natural gas instead of oil. Repsol produces crude oil and natural gas at a 34/66 ratio, i.e., its gas production is nearly double its oil production. As a result, the company is more resilient to an environment of low oil prices than oil producers.

The latest earnings report of the company did not have any surprises. In the first quarter of the year, Repsol saw its production decrease 3% sequentially due to some asset divestments, natural decline of its fields and some maintenance activity, but it grew its operating income 1% thanks to a marginal increase in the average price of Brent, higher gas prices and wider refining margins. It is important to note that the price of oil slumped after the end of the first quarter and hence its effect on the results of Repsol will show up in the next earnings report, which is expected in July.

With that said, it is critical to realize that the outlook of the natural gas market is highly positive. According to the latest report of the Energy Information Administration [EIA], thanks to strong demand for gas and nearly all-time high U.S. LNG exports, the average price of Henry Hub natural gas is expected to nearly double this year, from $2.20 in 2024 to $4.00 this year. Even better, EIA expects the average price of natural gas to climb another 22.5% next year, to $4.90. This forecast certainly bodes well for Repsol, which is highly sensitive to the price of natural gas.

Of course, the price of natural gas is significantly affected by the prevailing weather conditions, which can affect demand and inventories to a great extent. As the weather is unpredictable, forecasts about future gas prices are always surrounded by a high degree of uncertainty. Nevertheless, investors should be aware that the base scenario is highly positive for Repsol.

Dividend

As the stock of Repsol has rallied 20% this year, its dividend yield has decreased, but it remains above average, as it is currently standing at 7.6%. Even better, the dividend seems to have a wide margin of safety thanks to a combination of healthy conditions.

First of all, the trailing payout ratio (dividend to normalized earnings per share) is standing at 64% (1.09/1.70). This level is decent and is likely to improve, given the aforementioned favorable outlook of the natural gas market. In addition, Repsol has a remarkably strong balance sheet. It has a current ratio (= current assets / current liabilities) of 1.3x and net debt (as per Buffett's formula: Net debt = total liabilities - cash - receivables) of $20.5 billion. This amount is approximately 8 times the earnings of the company last year, and hence it is reasonable. More importantly, thanks to the low amount of debt of Repsol, its interest income exceeds its interest expense. As a result, the company is not burdened by any net interest expense. Very few companies are in such a strong financial condition.

Moreover, Repsol has provided guidance for 3% annual dividend hikes through 2027 even if the price of Brent decreases from its current level of $67 to $55 and the price of natural gas decreases from $3.54 to $3.0. Furthermore, the company is spending hefty amounts on share repurchases quarter after quarter. In the last 12 months, it has spent $1.3 billion on dividends and $1.1 billion on share repurchases. In the latest conference call, management reaffirmed its target for €700 million of share repurchases this year. A company that spends excessive amounts on share repurchases sends a strong signal that it does not struggle to maintain its dividend.

To cut a long story short, the 7.6% dividend of Repsol appears to have a wide margin of safety thanks to a decent payout ratio, a healthy balance sheet, a positive guidance of management and the confidence signaled by the aggressive share repurchases.

Valuation

Repsol has posted normalized earnings per share of $1.70 in the last 12 months. This means that the stock is currently trading at a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 8.4x. This earnings multiple is much lower than the median price-to-earnings ratio of 11.35x of the energy sector. Given also its exceptionally high dividend yield, which has a margin of safety, the stock appears attractive right now.

On the other hand, due to the high cyclicality of the oil and gas industry, Repsol is not a buy-and-hold forever stock. I recommend taking profits whenever the stock rallies up to $16-$17. This range has marked the peak of the stock in three instances in the last three years. Even when the prices of oil and gas skyrocketed to 13-year highs shortly after the invasion of Russia in Ukraine in 2022, the stock of Repsol did not exceed this range. Overall, this range appears reasonable for taking profits on Repsol.

Risk

As a commodity producer, Repsol is sensitive to any major downturn in the energy sector. If this summer is characterized by abnormally cool weather or next winter is exceptionally warm, gas prices are likely to decrease significantly. In such a case, the results of Repsol will be negatively affected.

Another risk factor is the unfavorable scenario of a global recession. If the U.S. government toughens its stance once again and imposes high tariffs on its trading partners, a global trade war may reemerge. If such a trade war causes a recession, it will significantly affect the global consumption of oil and gas and hence it is likely to exert pressure on the earnings of Repsol. Therefore, this stock is suitable only for patient investors, who can endure temporary setbacks and wait for a recovery.

Final Thoughts

The stock of Repsol has vastly outperformed the S&P 500 and the energy sector in the last six months, primarily thanks to its exceptionally cheap valuation six months ago and the positive outlook of the natural gas market. Despite its steep rally, the stock remains attractively valued, though it is not as cheap as it was late last year. Overall, I recommend holding the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Prudential: Still Attractive Valuation Despite Recent Share Price Run
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Despite Prudential (NYSE:PUK) strong share price performance year-to-date, the company is still trading below its embedded value. This implies that the market is applying a negative value to its new business profits. This doesn't make sense for a company like Prudential, which has a strong and leading insurance franchise in Asia, especially across the high-growth markets in the region. As such, I believe there could still be significant upside as the market continues to re-rate the stock, with its valuation catching up to that of its regional peer, AIA Group (OTCPK:AAGIY).

Background

After the demerger in 2021 to spin off its US and European businesses, Prudential is now a leading insurance company with businesses in life insurance and asset management focused purely on Asia and Africa. Asia accounts for the majority of its business (>70% of its APE sales in 2024).

The company's share price has finally risen by almost 50% year-to-date after several years of underperformance (down 54% from 2022 to 2024), especially compared to its regional life insurance peer, AIA Group (down 29% from 2022 to 2024).

Despite this recovery, its valuation still looks attractive, and I believe there's still ample potential upside as the recent share price performance is merely playing the catch-up.

Valuation Still Looks Unreasonably Cheap

Prudential is currently trading at 1.8x P/BV and remains at a 22% discount to AIA Group (2.3x).

P/BV (Prudential vs. AIA)

Two additional ways of assessing the valuation of life insurers involve examining the Embedded Value (EV) multiple and implied New Business multiple (NBM). This is where the divergence between Prudential and AIA becomes more apparent and why I believe Prudential's current valuation, despite its strong share price performance year-to-date, still looks unreasonably cheap and attractive.

Prudential is currently trading at 0.9x P/EV, i.e., below its embedded value. This implies the market is not assigning any value to its new business profits, based on the appraisal value approach that investors use to derive a life insurer's new business multiple.

Appraisal value (i.e., market capitalization) = Embedded Value (EV) + (new business multiple (NBM)) * (new business value/profits)

Prudential's implied new business multiple

Using this formula, we can see that the market is assigning a negative NBM (-1.5x) to Prudential's new business profits (vs. AIA's 4.6x).

Implied new business multiple (Prudential vs. AIA)

In my opinion, it's difficult to justify a negative NBM for Prudential, given the company's leading franchise in Asia, especially in high-growth markets in Southeast Asia such as Vietnam, Indonesia, the Philippines, and Singapore.

In fact, the company has been ranked among the top 5 life insurers across most of the key markets in Asia where it operates.

Company's leading presence in various markets

New Business Growth Continues to Recover to/Surpass Pre-Covid Levels

The company's new business sales and profits declined significantly during COVID-19, as it relies heavily on insurance sales to Mainland Chinese Visitors (MCV) in Hong Kong. Since the border between Hong Kong and mainland China was closed during Covid, its MCV business came to a halt and significantly impacted the group's overall new business sales and profits from 2020 to 2022.

Based on the company's FY24 results, its new business sales (measured in APE) in 2024 have already surpassed the pre-COVID level in 2019, while its new business profits have also nearly caught up with the 2019 level.

Prudential's APE (2019-2024)

Prudential's reported new business profits (2019-2024)

That said, the calculations of new business profits are subject to economic assumption changes. For example, the company's FY24 new business profits actually increased by 11% year-over-year (instead of declining ~2%) if we exclude the economic assumption changes (and FX movements) for the year. Hence, although the company's reported new business profits in 2024 were slightly lower than those in 2019, the recovery of its new business profits could actually be much stronger than the headline numbers suggest.

Looking ahead, management has also provided guidance for continued growth of the group's new business profits at a level of 15%-20% through 2027. Management is also confident about achieving the target of >$4.4 billion gross OFSG (operating free surplus generation) by 2027, which would continue to support its dividend payout (up 13% in 2024) and the share buyback program ($2 billion). All of these initiatives would help deliver further shareholder returns.

Management guidance and target

How Much Upside Could There Still Be?

Using the same appraisal value approach above, we can gauge the potential upside from the stock if its new business multiple continues to re-rate from the current -1.5x to just 0x (still unreasonable, frankly, for a company like Prudential). The following chart shows Prudential's NBM at the end of 2021 (after the demerger), 2022, 2023, and 2024.

Prudential's implied NBM (2021-2024)

One can see how the implied NBM declined from -0.1x (in the middle of Covid) at the end of 2021 to as low as -7.3x at the end of 2024 and is now being re-rated to -1.5x. Assuming no new business profits growth at all, continued NBM re-rating alone (to 0x) could imply a share price increase of 12%. If the NBM re-rates to AIA's current level (i.e., 4.6x), the implied upside could be as significant as almost 50%.

Upside calculation if NBM increases to 0x

Sensitivity table (NBM vs. new business profits growth rate)

Since management provided a target of 10%-20% new business profits growth, I believe the stock can likely offer additional upside of 10%-30% from the current level.

Risks

The primary risk centers around its reliance on Hong Kong and the MCV business. Looking at the new business profits mix by geography, we can understand how much the company relies on Hong Kong and, specifically, the MCV business within Hong Kong.

Before COVID, Hong Kong's contribution to the group's new business profits was 58% in 2019. The percentage dropped to as low as 20% during COVID due to border closures and has now returned to 47% in 2024. Any slowdown or interruption in its Hong Kong MCV business—for example, tightening policies from the Chinese government to restrict capital outflows (via insurance) to Hong Kong—would significantly impact the company's new business growth and valuation re-rating.

That said, thanks to COVID, the company has been working to further develop its presence and offerings in other markets, especially Singapore, after experiencing the downside of over-relying on a single market.

The contribution to the group's total new business profits from Singapore and markets defined as "Others" now stands at 40% in 2024 (vs. only 22% in 2019). Prudential's new business mix is now more balanced than ever.

New business profit mix by geography

Prudential's share price has been relatively volatile for an insurer of this size.

Its share price declined significantly from the beginning of 2022 to the end of 2024 and only managed to climb by almost 50% so far this year (outperforming both the S&P 500 and Hang Seng Index). Its share price is still down by almost 30% compared to the beginning of 2022.

Since the majority of its businesses are in Asia, especially in "Developing Asia" (e.g., India, Southeast Asia, mainland China) excluding Hong Kong and Singapore, current geopolitical tensions and trade wars can easily add much uncertainty to the trajectory of its share price recovery.

Conclusion

Despite Prudential's strong year-to-date performance, the stock remains undervalued, with the market applying a negative multiple to its new business profits. The company's leading position in high-growth Asian markets and improving operational metrics might suggest that the recent share price recovery is just the beginning of a broader re-rating process. Geopolitical risks and Hong Kong/MCV business concentration remain key concerns.
